metadata.title: Why Trying Harder to Sound Academic Makes Your Writing Less Academic
metadata.purpose: vox-explainer slate cut of Writing Guide candidate 09. Source chapter: writing-guide/chapters/05-bridging-the-divide-between-personal-identity-and-academia.md (the two failure modes: overshoot and refusal).
metadata.clock: narration (ElevenLabs) — durations below are word-count estimates until GATE 0 audio lock
metadata.color_semantics: CRIMSON = both failure modes (overshoot and refusal depart from the target); TEAL (plain ink) = the target zone — analytical work in the clearest possible sentences; never swap mid-film
metadata.note: Exclusions honored: NO Min-Zhan Lu translingual framework, NO full travels/doesn't-travel analysis, NO personal-to-academic arc of the full unit. Two failure modes only — overshoot (Latinate vocabulary, nominalized verbs, passive constructions) and refusal (treating conventions as betrayal). The fix for both: read in the genre you are about to write in.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: You sit down to write something academic. At the third word, the sentence feels wrong. Not wrong in any nameable way. Just wrong.
beats.scene_description: a student at a desk with a half-written page and a pen paused mid-sentence; revision marks visible; editorial desaturated treatment on newsprint ground; slow push-in
beats.image_prompt: B01, college student at a wooden desk, a single sheet of paper with two crossed-out opening sentences and a third barely begun, pen resting on the page, warm tungsten desk lamp from the left, overhead flat ambient, three-quarter angle from above-right, desaturated editorial treatment, no text overlays
beats.new_visual_element: [STILL] student paused mid-sentence — the wrongness feeling

beats.narration_text: The sentences that sound like you don't sound academic. The sentences that sound academic don't sound like you. You are standing at a border and the border is not marked.
beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] a line with 'sounds like you' left and 'sounds academic' right — gap in the middle

beats.narration_text: So here is the question: why does trying harder to sound academic often make writing less academic — and what are the two failure modes that catch writers on either side of the right register?

card.copy: Why does trying harder to sound academic make writing less academic?
card.sub: and what are the two failure modes?

beats.new_visual_element: [CARD] THE QUESTION — on screen and in narration

beats.narration_text: A student gets feedback: this doesn't sound academic. So they reach for what they imagine academic sounds like — Latinate words, passive constructions, sentences that delay their verb through three clauses.
beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] feedback arrow pointing toward Latinate features list

beats.narration_text: The second student takes the opposite route. Academic writing asks them to adapt their voice — and they read that as being asked to betray it. They refuse the translation entirely.
beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] refusal — writer turns away from the academic zone

beats.narration_text: Here is the spectrum. On one end: refuses translation. On the other: overshoots to jargon. In a narrow band in the middle: analytical work in the clearest possible sentences. That is what the academic register is actually for.
beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] horizontal spectrum — the central visual object of the film

beats.narration_text: The overshoot writer mistakes a signal of academic register for the work the register is designed to do. Strip the surface features off good academic prose — the work is still there. Strip them off overshoot — nothing is underneath.
beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] marker traces to OVERSHOOT — sentence transforms to jargon version

beats.narration_text: The refusal writer correctly senses that adaptation is required — but reads it as capitulation. The conventions of academic genres exist because a community agreed that those conventions make certain kinds of analytical work possible.
beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] marker traces to REFUSAL — conventions = work the genre does

beats.narration_text: Both writers are failing for the same structural reason: they are responding to a surface feature rather than understanding the underlying work. The marker ends up in the wrong place for opposite reasons.
beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] both failure markers shown simultaneously — symmetric failure

beats.narration_text: Consider one sentence at each point on the spectrum. The overshoot: 'the occurrence of hostilities was precipitated by the incapacity of diplomatic mechanisms to produce resolution.' The clear version: 'the war began because the diplomats ran out of moves.' The analysis is the same. The prose is not.
beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] two sentences at different spectrum positions — the worked example

beats.narration_text: The fix for both failures is the same: before you write in a genre, read carefully in it. Not to imitate — to understand what the conventions are actually doing. Once you know what they are for, you can use them intentionally.

card.copy: Read in the genre before you write in it.

beats.narration_text: Trying harder to sound academic makes writing less academic when the writer mistakes the surface features of the register for the analytical work the register is designed to do. The way out runs through the genre itself.

card.copy: The signal is not the work.

beats.new_visual_element: [CARD] RECAP — question to answer in one line
